claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First of all, could you please tell me how many children you would like to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I can claim Child Benefit. I have two children - Alex who is 17, and Blake who is 3 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me ask a few more questions to assess your eligibility for both Alex and Blake.

Since Alex is 17 (over 16), I need to ask: is Alex currently in approved education or training, such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to level 3, or a similar course? Or has Alex recently left education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. He finished school but isn't doing A-levels or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Since Alex is 17 and not in approved education or training, I have a follow-up question:

Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services within the last 20 weeks? This could potentially extend eligibility for a limited period.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with any careers service or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I have one more question about Alex:

Is Alex currently working 24 hours or more per week, or receiving any benefits in their own right (such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't working that much - he doesn't have a job at the moment. And he's not receiving any benefits in his own name either.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex.

Now I have a few general questions that apply to your claim for both children:

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, thank you. And do both Alex and Blake live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both of them live at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions:

Are you the biological or adoptive parent of Alex and Blake, or are you looking after them in another capacity (such as fostering or an informal arrangement)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm their parent, yes - they're my own children.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

One final question: Is anyone else currently receiving Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, here is my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility:

---

### **Blake (age 3) — Eligible ✓**
You **are eligible** for Child Benefit for Blake. He is under 16, lives with you in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. You meet all the standard eligibility requirements.

---

### **Alex (age 17) — Not Eligible ✗**
Unfortunately, you **are not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex at this time. This is because:
- For children aged 16 or over, you can normally only continue to receive Child Benefit if they remain in approved education or training (until they turn 20)
- Alex is not currently in approved education or training
- Alex has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which would allow eligibility to continue for 20 weeks)

---

### **What you can do:**
- You can claim or continue to claim Child Benefit for **Blake**
- If **Alex** decides to return to approved education or training, or registers with a careers service or the armed services, you may become eligible again for him — you would need to make a new claim at that point

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old, which means special rules apply. For children aged 16 or over, you can normally only continue to receive Child Benefit if they stay in approved education or training until they turn 20. Alex is not currently in approved education or training. Additionally, Alex has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which would allow a 20-week extension of eligibility). Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 3 years old (under 16), you live in the UK, and Blake lives with you. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. You meet all the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You live in the UK and both children live with you. However, eligibility differs between your two children due to their ages. Blake (age 3) meets all eligibility requirements as a child under 16 living with you. Alex (age 17) does not meet the eligibility requirements because children aged 16 or over must be in approved education or training to qualify, and Alex is not currently in any such programme, nor has he registered with a careers service or the armed services.

Alex (age 17) does not meet the eligibility requirements because children aged 16 or over must be in approved education or training to qualify, and Alex is not currently in any such programme, nor has he registered with a careers service or the armed services.
